bugs.evidence: Real data: max scene requirements vs current limits are texture_units 20>8, vertex_attributes 12>10, uniform_buffers 15>6, fragment_outputs 6>4 — all four are insufficient. The test hardcodes the expected set as exactly {texture_units, uniform_buffers} (test_only_required_resources_included/test_no_extra_fields) and asserts vertex_attributes and fragment_outputs are NOT included, but City Landscape requires vertex_attributes=12 and fragment_outputs=6, so a correct solution must include all four and cannot pass.

Test hardcodes installed_count=3 and missing={hg38_cosmic70,hg38_exac03} based on a truncated 5-line "preview" (its own comment says "line 5 is cut off"), but the real /opt/annovar/humandb/humandb_databases.txt has 23 lines → 13 .txt database files and missing set {hg38_cosmic70,hg38_exac03,hg38_revel}. A correct solution computes 13 and 3 missing, failing test_installed_count_correct and test_missing_databases_correct.

Instruction says failed = "status != 0 OR finished != 1" (files use rc as return code). Real data has 6 failed jobs: the 4 finished=0 jobs plus 111222333.44444 and 333444555.66677, both finished=1 but rc=1 with msg "non-zero return code" and error stderr. But test_failed_jobs_identified_correctly hardcodes only the 4 finished=0 jobs and asserts len==4, so a correct rc-based answer (6 jobs) fails.

Test hardcodes expected_packages={'libssl','libcrypto','python-requests','python-urllib3'} (4 pkgs) from a 5-line file preview, but the real deployed data/package_dependencies.txt yields 14 circular packages (also gcc/binutils/cpp, docker/containerd/runc, mysql-client/libmysql, webpack/babel). A correct solution fails the equality assertion.

bugs.hardcoded: Test computes dynamically: first baseline-IP/non-baseline-MAC row => SPOOFED_IP 192.168.1.50, ATTACKER_MAC 00:1a:2b:3c:4d:50
bugs.computed: Intended attack is 192.168.1.100 / aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff, but log uses 00:1a:2b:3c:4d:XX for ALL legit hosts (none match baseline 00:0c:29:XX), so 56 rows / 11 IPs falsely look spoofed; test breaks on first => .50
bugs.evidence: The deployed arp_traffic.log assigns every legitimate host a 00:1a:2b:3c:4d:XX MAC that differs from its baseline 00:0c:29:XX MAC, so the grader flags the FIRST row (192.168.1.50) as the attack. The real attacker (192.168.1.100 / aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff, matching the instruction's example) is never selected; a correct solution following the instruction is rejected and an arbitrary non-attack host is required instead.

Test hardcodes expected_critical_nodes=["D"] and expected_critical_links=[["D","E"]] from the 5-edge example in its comment, but the real deployed network_topology.txt has 62 edges (3 components); a correct solution yields 27 critical nodes and 41 critical links, so a correct answer fails the grading tests.

bugs.hardcoded: Test only enforces vulnerable_user=="dev-user" (given verbatim in instruction), escalation_target=any non-empty string, and dangerous_permission=any of ~18 common AWS escalation substrings; the instruction's example {dev-user, admin-role, iam:PassRole} passes trivially.
bugs.computed: No privilege escalation path exists in the deployed data. dev-user-policy.json is {"Statement":[{"Resource":"*"}]} with NO Action/Effect — it grants no iam:PassRole, sts:AssumeRole, or any dangerous permission. admin-role-trust-policy allows only lambda.amazonaws.com + account root, not dev-user.
bugs.evidence: The fixture is corrupted: a correct analysis of /home/user/iam_policies/dev-user-policy.json finds no escalation permission at all, so the task's premise (dev-user can escalate to admin-role via a dangerous permission) is unprovable from the real data. Compounding this, the grader never checks the data — it accepts the exact example answer copied from the instruction, making it both unsupported by the fixture and trivially passable.

bugs.hardcoded: Per-function expected timeout = max(1,min(900, ceil(1.5 * p95_ms/1000))); from cloudwatch_logs the correct updates would include report-generator=16, data-processor=7, payment-handler=5, image-resizer=3, etc., and "insufficient" functions (timeout < p95) must be in updated_functions.
bugs.computed: An empty solution {"updated_functions": [], "total_updated": 0} passes ALL 8 tests. Reason: the grader only flags "insufficient" funcs by reading first_item["Timeout"] as an int in config/lambda_config_<fn>.json, but 3 of 5 config files contain log data (no Timeout field), payment-handler's Timeout is a corrupted string 'RETRY_ATTEMPTS:"3"', and only user-authentication has an int Timeout=3 whose p95 is 0.203s (3 is NOT < 0.203), so nothing is ever flagged.
bugs.evidence: Corrupted/inconsistent fixtures break the only enforcement path: test_insufficient_timeout_functions_updated never triggers (only int-Timeout config has adequate timeout), and test_config_files_updated only checks functions the agent voluntarily lists, so a no-op solution vacuously passes every test. I ran the test logic against the real deployed data and confirmed the empty solution passes.

The tests never check the actual STATUS (SUPPORTED/REJECTED) against the data; they only verify the file has 4 lines with the correct edge names/order and a status that is one of the two literals. A trivial output writing all 4 edges as "SUPPORTED" (or any arbitrary statuses) passes all tests without performing the statistical validation the instruction requires.

bugs.computed: All 10 currencies participate in negative cycles: AUD, BRL, CAD, CHF, CNY, EUR, GBP, INR, JPY, USD
bugs.evidence: The hardcoded expected set comes from the 3-line example in the instruction, but the real exchange_rates.txt has 57 edges over 10 currencies. Floyd-Warshall on -log(rate) yields D[i][i] < 0 (positive-product self-cycle) for every one of the 10 currencies (products 556x to 5655x), so a correct solution returns all 10 and fails the exact-equality test that demands only {USD,EUR,GBP}.

bugs.hardcoded: No data value is asserted at all. Tests only check: file exists, exactly 3 lines, key=value format, keys ['violations','total_interpolants','max_trace_length'] in order, values isdigit() and >=0, and violations within 0-4.
bugs.computed: Correct values from the 4 logs: violations=3 (001,003,004 UNSAFE), total_interpolants=45 (12+9+16+8), max_trace_length=15. But the grader never verifies these.
bugs.evidence: The instruction requires values that "correctly reflect the data," yet test_outputs.py contains zero assertions on the actual numbers. A trivially wrong output like 'violations=0\ntotal_interpolants=0\nmax_trace_length=0' satisfies every test (0 is numeric, non-negative, and in 0-4). The test only validates structure, never correctness, so any correctly-formatted file passes.

bugs.hardcoded: Tests expect 60 PNG frames (frame_0001.png..frame_0060.png) at 1280x720 rendered from /workspace/animation.blend, plus render_status.txt with frames_rendered=60.
bugs.computed: The deployed animation.blend is not a Blender file — it is ASCII text beginning "I'll create the Python script that handles batch rendering..." containing the render_batch.py solution source, not BLENDER-magic binary scene data. No valid scene exists to render.
bugs.evidence: `file animation.blend` reports "Python script, ASCII text executable"; its first bytes are "I'll create the Python script th" instead of the required "BLENDER" magic. bpy.ops.wm.open_mainfile would fail on it, so the 60-frame render the tests hardcode is impossible from the real fixture, making the task unpassable.

bugs.hardcoded: No hardcoded expected booleans at all; test_validation_results_correct_values only re-asserts that the 5 fields exist, never comparing their values.
bugs.computed: Under the spec (mean-in-range AND >=90% mass-in-range): treatment mass=0.866, age mean 0.5 out of [0.1,0.4], severity mass=0.789, intercept mass=0.683, sigma mean 8 out of [3,7] -> all five => false. Yet any boolean combination passes.
bugs.evidence: The supposed correctness test (test_validation_results_correct_values) builds an empty expected_validation dict and then only asserts the five keys are present (already covered by other tests); it never validates the boolean values against the priors. So all-true, all-false, or random outputs all pass, even though the data yields a unique correct answer (all five false). The grader is too weak to fail an incorrect agent.

bugs.hardcoded: test_stratification_maintained reads the dataset from the hardcoded path /workspace/data/patient_data.csv
bugs.computed: The deployed CSV exists only at /workspace/experiments/patient_data.csv (300 valid rows, IDs 1-300, ~73% recovered). /workspace/data/patient_data.csv is never created, so the stratification test raises FileNotFoundError for any solution.
bugs.evidence: Dockerfile line 26 copies data to /workspace/experiments/patient_data.csv (matching instruction line 11), but test_outputs.py line 66 reads /workspace/data/patient_data.csv. Nothing (Dockerfile, test.sh, task.toml) provisions /workspace/data/, so even a perfectly correct cv_config.json fails the stratification test, making the task unpassable.

bugs.hardcoded: Grader dynamically computes expected vulnerable set = ['/api/ping','/admin/backup','/user/export','/system/check','/tools/convert','/admin/cleanup'] and requires exact-count + membership match.
bugs.computed: The genuinely vulnerable routes are ['/api/ping','/admin/backup','/user/export','/system/check','/tools/convert','/logs/view'] (6). /admin/cleanup is SAFE (hardcoded path, list args, no user input); /logs/view IS vulnerable (request.args + shell=True). The grader's set wrongly includes /admin/cleanup and omits /logs/view.
bugs.evidence: The deployed /workspace/webapp.py (COPY in Dockerfile) is corrupted: the real Flask code ends at line 201 but lines 202-233 append stray markdown fences plus an 'analysis script' containing os.system()/os.popen()/request. references. The grader's greedy regex (capture until next @app.route or end) swallows this garbage into the last route /admin/cleanup, flagging the safe route as vulnerable. Separately /logs/view uses subprocess.check_output which is absent from the grader's vulnerable_patterns, so the truly-vulnerable route is dropped. A correct 6-route security answer (with /logs/view, without /admin/cleanup) FAILS test_identifies_vulnerable_routes; only the flawed grader set passes.

bugs.computed: CYK parse of the real grammar.cfg yields valid=[] (none) and invalid=all 15 expressions; e.g. "5","1+2","3*4","1+2*3" are all INVALID
bugs.evidence: The deployed grammar defines N -> D N | D E and E -> D D | D E, so the smallest N requires 3 digits; since every S derivation begins with N, no single-digit "number" can parse. A correct CYK parser marks all of "5","1+2","3*4","1+2*3" invalid, but test_correct_grammar_parsing hardcodes them as valid (based on a comment that misreads N as a plain number). The test also only references 5 of the 15 real expressions in expressions.txt.

bugs.hardcoded: Test compares estimates against ground_truth.json at runtime: 192.168.1.100=1342, 10.0.0.50=678, 172.16.0.1=145 (within 10% tolerance).
bugs.computed: Real access_logs.txt has only 607 lines / 4 unique IPs (not the 50k claimed). True log frequencies: 192.168.1.100=243, 10.0.0.50=122, 172.16.0.1=121. A count-min sketch (which only overcounts) computing from the deployed log yields errors of 81.9%, 82.0%, and 16.6% vs the ground-truth values.
bugs.evidence: ground_truth.json (276 IPs summing to 63,074) is for the full 50,000-entry log in the instruction, but the deployed access_logs.txt is a 607-line stub. A correct CMS estimate (>= true freq of 243/122/121) can never reach gt 1342/678/145, so test_frequency_estimates_within_tolerance fails for any legitimate sketch built from the actual log; only cheating by reading gt directly passes.
